fragments.content: Schizophrenia is a chronic psychotic disorder characterized by disturbances in thought, perception, emotion, and behavior. Core symptoms include positive symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech/behavior), negative symptoms (avolition, alogia, anhedonia, flat affect, social withdrawal), and cognitive impairments (attention, memory, executive function). Onset typically occurs in late adolescence/early adulthood. Pathophysiology involves dopamine hyperactivity in mesolimbic pathways (positive symptoms) and hypoactivity in mesocortical pathways (negative/cognitive symptoms), glutamate NMDA receptor dysfunction, neurodevelopmental abnormalities, and genetic factors (heritability ~80%).

First-line treatment: second-generation antipsychotics (SGAs) like risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine, aripiprazole, or first-generation (FGAs) like haloperidol. Choice depends on side effect profile, patient preference, and prior response. Clozapine is reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (failure of ≥2 antipsychotics) with superior efficacy but requires monitoring for agranulocytosis. Long-acting injectables improve adherence. Psychosocial interventions include CBT for psychosis, family psychoeducation, supported employment, and social skills training. Early intervention programs improve long-term outcomes. Regular monitoring for metabolic syndrome, extrapyramidal symptoms, and tardive dyskinesia is essential.

DSM-5-TR criteria: ≥2 symptoms for ≥1 month (or less if successfully treated), with at least one being delusions, hallucinations, or disorganized speech; other symptoms include grossly disorganized/catatonic behavior or negative symptoms. Continuous signs persist for ≥6 months including ≥1 month of active symptoms plus prodromal/residual periods. Significant functional impairment in work, relationships, or self-care. Schizoaffective and mood disorders ruled out. Substance/medical causes excluded. Specifiers include first episode vs multiple episodes, acute episode status, severity, and presence of catatonia. Course specifiers: episodic with/without residual symptoms, or continuous.
